description: Cloud Engineering Glossary — 60+ fundamental terms organized by domain. Every term includes definition, also-known-as, related terms, and module references.

entries.definition: On-demand delivery of compute power, database, storage, applications, and other IT resources through a cloud services platform via the internet with pay-as-you-go pricing.
Cloud computing model that provides virtualized computing resources over the internet. You manage the OS, middleware, and applications; the provider manages the hardware.
Cloud computing model that provides a platform allowing customers to develop, run, and manage applications without the complexity of building and maintaining infrastructure.
Cloud computing model where software is licensed on a subscription basis and centrally hosted. Users access it via a web browser.
Security model defining which security tasks are handled by the cloud provider and which are handled by the customer. The provider secures the cloud; you secure what's in the cloud.
A geographical area containing one or more datacenters networked together with low-latency connections. Resources deployed in a region stay within that region unless you explicitly replicate them.
Physically separate datacenters within a region, each with independent power, cooling, and networking. Deploying across zones protects against datacenter-level failures.
Platform for developing, shipping, and running applications in containers. Docker packages an application and its dependencies into a standardized unit called a container.
A lightweight, standalone, executable package that includes everything needed to run software: code, runtime, system tools, libraries, and settings. Containers share the host OS kernel.
Open-source container orchestration platform that automates deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications across clusters of hosts.
The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. A Pod encapsulates one or more containers that share storage, network, and a specification for how to run them.
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool that lets you define cloud resources using declarative configuration files. It manages resource lifecycle: create, update, delete.
Practice of managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable definition files rather than physical hardware configuration or interactive tools.
Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery/Deployment. CI: automatically building and testing code changes. CD: automatically deploying those changes to production.
Operating model where Git repositories are the single source of truth for declarative infrastructure and applications. Automated processes ensure the actual state matches the desired state in Git.
Declarative GitOps continuous delivery tool for Kubernetes. It monitors Git repositories for changes and automatically syncs the cluster state to match.
Set of practices combining software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops) to shorten the development lifecycle and provide continuous delivery with high software quality.

Discipline that applies software engineering principles to infrastructure and operations. Focuses on creating scalable and highly reliable software systems through automation, SLOs, and error budgets.
A target value or range for a service level measured by an SLI. Example: '99.9% of requests complete in under 200ms over a 30-day window.'
The ability to understand a system's internal state by examining its outputs: metrics, logs, and traces. Goes beyond monitoring by enabling exploration of unknown-unknowns.
Open-source monitoring and alerting toolkit designed for reliability. It collects and stores metrics as time series data with a powerful query language (PromQL).
Open-source platform for monitoring and observability. It allows you to query, visualize, alert on, and understand your metrics through rich dashboards.

Cloud financial management discipline that enables organizations to get maximum business value from cloud spending through cross-functional collaboration between engineering, finance, and business teams.
Security model that assumes no trust by default — every access request must be authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated, regardless of where it originates.
Framework of policies and technologies ensuring the right individuals access the right resources at the right times for the right reasons.
Method of regulating access to resources based on the roles of individual users within an organization. Permissions are assigned to roles, not directly to users.
Technique that enhances LLM responses by retrieving relevant information from a knowledge base before generating an answer. Grounds AI outputs in factual data.
Database optimized for storing and querying high-dimensional vectors (embeddings). Used for semantic search, recommendation systems, and AI applications.
Numerical representation of data (text, images, audio) as a vector in high-dimensional space. Similar items have similar vectors, enabling semantic comparison.
AI model trained on vast text corpora that can understand, generate, and manipulate human language. Examples: GPT-4, Claude, Llama, Gemini.
Practice of designing and optimizing prompts to effectively interact with LLMs. Involves crafting instructions, examples, and context to get desired outputs.
Set of practices combining Machine Learning, DevOps, and Data Engineering to deploy and maintain ML models in production reliably and efficiently.
Specialized processor originally designed for graphics rendering, now essential for AI/ML workloads due to its ability to perform massive parallel computations.
Package manager for Kubernetes. Helm Charts define, install, and upgrade even the most complex Kubernetes applications through templated configuration.
Discipline of designing and building toolchains and workflows that enable self-service capabilities for software engineering organizations. Builds Internal Developer Platforms (IDPs).
Integration of security practices within the DevOps process. Shifts security left in the development lifecycle rather than treating it as a final gate.
Distributed version control system that tracks changes in source code during software development. Designed for speed, data integrity, and support for distributed workflows.
Method for allocating IP addresses and routing. CIDR notation (e.g., 10.0.0.0/16) specifies a network prefix and the number of significant bits, defining the network size.
Hierarchical naming system that translates human-readable domain names (e.g., google.com) to machine-readable IP addresses (e.g., 142.250.80.46).
Cryptographic protocol designed to provide communications security over a computer network. Successor to SSL. Used for HTTPS, secure email, and VPNs.
Device or service that distributes network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server bears too much demand, improving responsiveness and availability.
Deployment and management service for Azure. Provides a consistent management layer to create, update, and delete resources in your Azure subscription.
Logical container for Azure resources. Resources in a group share the same lifecycle, permissions, and policies. Deleting a resource group deletes all contained resources.
Managed Kubernetes service on Azure that simplifies deploying, managing, and operating Kubernetes clusters by offloading much of the operational overhead to Azure.
Microsoft's cloud-based identity and access management service. Provides single sign-on, multi-factor authentication, and conditional access for applications.
Microsoft Azure service providing access to OpenAI's powerful language models (GPT-4, DALL-E) with Azure's enterprise-grade security, compliance, and regional availability.
Open-source observability framework for generating, collecting, and exporting telemetry data (traces, metrics, logs). Vendor-neutral standard for instrumentation.
Microsoft's unified monitoring solution for Azure and hybrid environments. Collects, analyzes, and acts on telemetry data from cloud and on-premises environments.
CI/CD platform integrated into GitHub that allows automation of build, test, and deployment pipelines directly from GitHub repositories.
Terraform state file that maps real-world resources to your configuration. Critical for tracking resource metadata and enabling Terraform to know what to create, update, or delete.
The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. A Pod encapsulates one or more containers that share storage, network namespace, and a specification for how to run them.
Learning technique that involves actively stimulating memory during the learning process rather than passively reviewing material. Testing yourself is more effective than re-reading.
Evidence-based learning technique where review sessions are spaced over increasing intervals of time. Exploits the psychological spacing effect for better long-term retention.
Learning method that involves explaining a concept in simple terms as if teaching it to someone else. Identifies gaps in understanding when you can't explain something simply.
Set of guiding tenets for building secure, high-performing, resilient, and efficient infrastructure. Pillars: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization.
Dedicated infrastructure layer for handling service-to-service communication in microservices. Provides observability, traffic management, and security without changing application code.
Deployment strategy using two identical environments (blue = current, green = new). Traffic is switched to green after validation. Enables instant rollback by switching back to blue.
Four key metrics identified by Google's DORA team for measuring software delivery performance: Deployment Frequency, Lead Time for Changes, Change Failure Rate, Time to Restore Service.
Azure AD feature that uses if-then statements to enforce organizational policies for accessing resources. Example: 'If user is outside the office, require MFA.'
